rationale: The citing paper uses LeBoeuf (2006) as core prior evidence that duration-versus-date framing changes intertemporal judgments, and it places this work in the "more closely related" literature used to motivate its main prediction. The cited study is also used for mechanism-relevant expectations (e.g., duration descriptions potentially lengthening perceived intervals) and for interpreting why some of the new results differ from prior findings. In the general discussion, the authors explicitly state they are extending prior time-description research, including LeBoeuf (2006), to a new decision domain (goal adoption). This is more than a passing mention because it informs framing, hypothesis development, and contribution positioning.
relation_description: The citing paper builds on LeBoeuf (2006) by taking the same duration-versus-date interval framing idea into a different outcome domain: goal-pursuit adoption decisions rather than discounting. It adds a new process-versus-outcome focus account and boundary conditions while positioning itself as an extension of that earlier time-description line of work.
